county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tillman County formed 1907 at statehood from Comanche County (KCA lottery area). County seat is Frederick. Named for Benjamin Tillman. Cotton-farming county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tillman County was created at statehood (1907) from part of Comanche County in the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache lottery area (1901). For pre-1907 records, check Comanche County. NARA Fort Worth holds tribal allotment records for the KCA area. County Clerk records in Frederick begin from 1907. BLM GLO land patents document original settler claims. Cotton farming was the primary industry. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma county marriage records. The Tillman County Historical Society and Frederick Public Library have local genealogical materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Tillman County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tulsa County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Tulsa, the 'Oil Capital of the World.' Site of the 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre (Greenwood District / 'Black Wall Street').
county_metadata.research_tips: Tulsa County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory and became the 'Oil Capital of the World' in the early 20th century. Creek Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records at NARA Fort Worth are critical for tribal research. The Tulsa Race Massacre of 1921 devastated the Greenwood District ('Black Wall Street'), destroying records and displacing thousands of African American residents -- the 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre Centennial Commission and the Greenwood Cultural Center have collected survivor testimonies and documentation. The Tulsa City-County Library has an exceptional genealogy department with Tulsa city directories, newspaper indexes, and the Beryl Ford Collection of photographs. Oil company records, corporate records, and professional directories document the oil boom. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and court records. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Gilcrease Museum has Native American and Western history collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tulsa County Clerk land records; Creek Nation allotment records at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land

source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Wagoner County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Wagoner. Named for the Missouri-Kansas-Texas (Katy) railroad dispatcher 'Bigfoot' Wagoner.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wagoner County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. Creek Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records are critical. NARA Fort Worth holds Creek Agency and Union Agency records. Some Cherokee Nation records may also be relevant for the northern portion. The county had All-Black communities and freedmen families. County Clerk records in Wagoner begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Wagoner County Genealogical Society has local materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Wagoner County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Washington County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Bartlesville. Named for George Washington. Site of Oklahoma's first commercial oil well (1897).
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. Bartlesville, the county seat, was the site of Oklahoma's first commercial oil well (1897). Cherokee Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls, and allotment records are critical. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency records. Phillips Petroleum Company was headquartered in Bartlesville -- company records may document employees. The Bartlesville Area History Museum has local collections. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Washington County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions. The Dewey Hotel Museum has local materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Washington County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Washita County formed 1892 from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands. County seat is New Cordell (often called Cordell). Named for the Washita River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washita County was organized from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands (1892). County Clerk records in New Cordell begin from 1892. BLM GLO land patents document original settler claims from the Cheyenne-Arapaho opening. NARA Fort Worth holds Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency records. The county was predominantly agricultural with cotton and wheat farming. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. The Washita County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. The Washita Valley area attracted German and Czech settlers -- check church records (Catholic and Lutheran parishes).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Washita County; County Clerk deed books from 1892

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Cherokee Outlet run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Woods County formed 1893 from the Cherokee Outlet (land run). County seat is Alva, home to Northwestern Oklahoma State University. Alfalfa, Harper, and Major counties carved from it at statehood.
county_metadata.research_tips: Woods County was formed from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. Alva, the county seat, is home to Northwestern Oklahoma State University. County Clerk records begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims. Alfalfa, Harper, and Major counties were later carved from Woods at statehood -- for post-1907 records, check the correct successor county. The Cherokee Strip Museum in Alva has pioneer family records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. Northwestern Oklahoma State University library has regional history collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. The Woods County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Woods County; County Clerk deed books from 1893

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Cherokee Outlet run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Woodward County formed 1893 from the Cherokee Outlet (land run). County seat is Woodward. Major cattle shipping point. Devastating tornado killed over 100 people in 1947.
county_metadata.research_tips: Woodward County was formed from the Cherokee Outlet, opened by land run on September 16, 1893. Woodward was a major cattle shipping point on the railroad. County Clerk records begin from 1893. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims. The Plains Indians and Pioneers Museum in Woodward has local history materials. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. A devastating tornado struck Woodward in 1947, killing over 100 people -- records of the disaster and its victims are locally held. Harper County was carved from Woodward at statehood. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. Cattle industry records (brand registrations, livestock associations) are important for family research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Woodward County; County Clerk deed books from 1893
